item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, and consider it a classic, but I really do wish that more was done with these characters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was initially confused by the other comments here, cause the mention of "physical altercations" and the list of -1's injuries made me think it was an abusive relationship and a metaphor for domestic abuse.

But after looking at it a bit closer… yeah, now I see what people are talking about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also topical for Valentine's Day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still super underrated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted because this is really cute concept, and also because I really hope something will actually be done with it in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of like it. It is cute and everything.

I like to see that the Foundation neither is being overly skeptical ("It can't be a ghost. We know what a ghost look like and this ain't it") nor jumps to the conclusion ("We got a poltergeist entity! Code Nice-and-Purple! Code Nice-and-Purple").

Personally, the main attractive I find for this skip is the fact that the guy could be a reality bender and he is so deluded thinking his boyfriend is a ghost, he made him into a ghost. Is both heartbreaking and heartwarming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where's the sex?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unfortunately, this hits Category #2 of things I almost universally dislike for SCPs:

Weird, Benign Sex Stuff

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. It's nice and… cosy, perhaps? And I like the Foundation not wanting to disturb the two poor guys with more tests.

I have two questions:
- why does the Foundation consider SCP-2240-1 as an SCP? He isn't harming anyone (except himself by taking drugs, if I understood right), and his 'ghost' cannot harm anyone else either…
- this:

When shown photographs of the late ██████ ████ taken shortly before his death, SCP-2240-1 asserts that the appearance of SCP-2240-2 is largely identical.

reads weird, I think. Of course the guy would say that, whether he's a fraud, a reality-bender or a haunted man. Wouldn't it be more logical to have the Foundation ask 'So, what does this ghost look like?' and the guy whip up a picture of his dead lover?

Upvoted anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall, I like it. The concept is nice, not creepy/squicky, but very human/psychological. The character is a little wooden, but not unbelievable. I've got a couple of questions:

SCP-2240-2 is a spacetime anomaly of an as yet undetermined nature, completely imperceptible by all non-anomalous Foundation technology…

So have we considered any more anomalous options? I know cross-testing is discouraged, but there have been references in tales to Foundation-developed anomalous technology. I think determining whether or not it has a form is something that we'd want to know.

Why haven't we done a relayed interview with -2? I got the impression that -2 can "hear" what the Foundation researchers are saying; why haven't we done more with this? It's understood that the information would be suspect, but could be used to help determine if -1 is suffering the stresses of having to lie on the fly. Hell, hook him up to a polygraph. "Tell us something SCP-2240-1 wouldn't know." Color drains out of SCP-2240-1s face. He then proceeds to get very angry. They are observed 'fighting' for a period of ?? days, before reconciliation."

The tests mentioned above by Argenti make sense. Range of effect (or lack of a definable range) would be cool to know.

Just my .02. +1 for concept, hoping it becomes all it can be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Proof that SCP can have small stories that lead nowhere in particular. Not every anomalous shit can destroy the world. Some of them simply haunt their couples and bite them in the neck

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is cute but I agree with other commenters that there's not quite enough stuff going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, well-put, that's exactly what I was thinking. This is a good starting point, but only that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. That note at the end seems less like the foundation making a rational decision and more like a cop-out to avoid writing an experiment log. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FWIW, I think this is really well done. Upvoted. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept isn't a bad idea. Poltergeist boyfriend haunting someone… sure, that works for me.

But it doesn't really go anywhere, and what's here is just okay. The dialogue is a bit goofy — you probably wanna cut the howling with laughter bit in particular. The character doesn't feel real - more like a parody.

The Description is also a problem. Overall it's good tone, but it's fairly long and unnecessarily hard to read. I've certainly had that problem in my own writing… but it's still a problem.

Overall, it's in no-vote territory for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"as well as four (4) bruised bite marks to the left side of the neck, regarding which SCP-2240-1 has not been forthcoming." XD

Too bad it doesn't really go anywhere, cuz I think it's kinda sweet. For some ideas — plenty of non-invasive tests into -2's range are possible, stupid things like seeing how far away they can put the water cooler and still have him get -1 a drink for example. Some of the standard ESP tests might be interesting too — have -2 see the cards, but -1 not, and have -1 answer what's on them (there's a set of cards somebody made for this, but I can't remember wtf they're called).

The other thing you could do would be to take gay stereotypes and turn them on their head, but idk how well that could be worked into a clinical tone.

No vote, because I do like it, but think you could do more with it.

Edit: you could also play up the psych aspect — how glad he is to be believed instead of dosed with anti-psychotics (some of which can have some nasty side effects you could maybe use).

As usual, thedeadlymoose nailed it. This is a person, haunted by his dead lover, getting to continue that relationship after death, who's been on all sorts of drugs, arrested, thought crazy (by himself even); there's so much here to work with, but he feels like a cardboard cutout, not a person.

Also, the note reads like lolFoundation. We got that they're hickeys, no need to spell it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've read this draft and i did think you'd give it some work before actually publishing. It's not ready as it is, and also, O4 is not a thing. Clearances are given through levels, 4 being top secret (for site directors and MTFs) and 5 being Thaumiel (Overseers and other high value personnel). O5 is a designation, not a clearance level.

Downvote. No vote. I think you can fix this in time and you should go for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the idea of homosexual ghost kinky relationship. That is hella cute.

However, nothing is done with them. It's like having interesting characters and an interesting premise/setting but then deciding that no plot was necessary and leaving it as is. It's a crying shame. No vote with great hopes that something is done with this couple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the abbreviations are a bigger sticking point than redundancy, I'll fill them all out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm. I'm not really sure.

The concept's all right. Tone, writing, procedures, and description is pretty good for a first skip. The concept is weird, but it doesn't make me really that confused or creeped out, which I personally feel can be done better with an idea like this.

I am novoting, though, just because of two things that can't make me up vote. One is 2240-2's description, which could be really creative but it just feels rushed and glossed over. It's very easy to say, 'spatial anomaly that we can barely detect' but I dunno, is that all? Maybe adding more info to the nature of 2240-2 could give this article a little more flesh on its bones, which it needs.

Second is the interview. I understand the guy is supposed to be obtuse, but what does it really add to the article other than a few "lol we're gay" jokes? I mean, yeah, he says he sees his boyfriend, he says medicine didn't work, but what can you add that really sells the article?

As for usage of -1 and -2, just use SCP-2240-1 and SCP-2240-2. People will more likely downvote for usage of the former than redundancy of the latter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I originally had it as SCP-2240-1 and SCP-2240-2 throughout, but (and this is my fault) I ended up referencing them way too often, and seeing SCP-2240-1 and SCP-2240-2 repeated ad nauseam looked bad. I asked in #site19 and someone said it'd be fine to abbreviate to -1 and -2 for multiple uses in the same paragraph.

The dialogue is coming from a twenty-something South Floridian pill head who as far as he knows got transferred from one jail to a better jail because of his boyfriend (who may be a bona fide ghost or may just be the hallucination of a delusional and untalented reality bender.) I thought the dialogue was appropriate for that. I was going to add a Site Director note at one point, but TwistedGears in #site19 persuaded me that it was too on-the-nail about the concept. Though the finished version might actually have gone a bit far in the other direction…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The -1s and -2s should be changed to SCP-2240-1 and SCP-2240-2, and the interview dialogue sounds a little too casual. In general, interview logs use the word "doc" way too often, your SCP is just the most recent to. Other than that it's not as especially original concept (Dude's being haunted. It's been done.) but it's executed okay. No vote from me.
